The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

{{Shrine | Name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other Names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:new pages]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution 2]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[category{Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Pages needing attribution]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Pages needing attribution]] [[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:125 shrines of Ise needing history import]] [[Category:125 shrines of Ise]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:125 shrines of Ise]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

revision.comment: Immanuelle moved page [[Kamiumi Shrine]] to [[Kami-no-Mii Shrine]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:New pages]] [[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kamiumi Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well Deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:qq]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:qq]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[ja:上御井神社]]

Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

{{Shrine | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Kami-no-Mii-no Shrine pray spot.jpg|270px|center|Prayer spot at Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|ja|三重県}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|ja|神嘗祭}} (October 19) }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyo'uke Daijingu|ja|豊受大神宮}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Grand Shrine|ja|伊勢神宮}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[ja:上御井神社]]

The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|ja|伊勢市}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|ja|玉垣|Tamagaki=Tamagaki|lt=wooden fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|kami|ja|神}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|ja|高天原}} to {{ill|Hyuga Province|ja|日向国}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tamba Province|ja|丹波国}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Otsu Shrine (Ise)|ja|大津神社}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|JR Sangu Line|ja|参宮線}} or {{ill|Kintetsu Yamada Line|ja|近鉄山田線}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} [[Category:Ise City Shrines]] [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:Japanese Wells]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-25]] [[Category:category refresh]] [[Category:Kk]] [[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq---qq]] [[Category:qq-Kami-no-Mii Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:bump 1-27]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:properly attributed pages]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:properly attributed pages]] [[Category:refreshing so the massreplace can be done again]] [[Category:kk]][[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]][[Category:kk]] [[Category:Kami-no-Mii Shrine]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:NAMESPACE]] [[Category:subst:MAINSPACE]] [[ja:上御井神社]]
