It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|qq=}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=JR Sangu Line|1=Sangū Line}} or {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line|1=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mi Shrine|1=Q11488769|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488769}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|上御井神社|version=97910291|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:上御井神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17226402}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Completion tier 4]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water wells in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

[[ja:上御井神社]] [[ja:上御井神社]] [[ja:上御井神社]] [[ja:上御井神社]] {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Kami-no-Mii Shrine (上御井神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = File:Geku Miyanomegurinokami.jpg | location = {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo River Town|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, {{ill|Mie Prefecture|en|Mie Prefecture|WD=Q128196|lt=Mie Prefecture|1=Mie Prefecture}} | Latitude = 34 | Latitude_Min = 29 | Latitude_Sec = 15.3 | Longitude = 136 | Longitude_Min = 41 | Longitude_Sec = 59.4 | Map = Japan Mie | Enshrined Kami = Guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神) | type = {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} subordinate shrine | Established = Unknown | Honden = None | Other names = Oshio-i (忍穂井) | Notable Festivals = {{ill|Kannamesai Festival|en|Kannamesai Festival|WD=Q11255045|lt=Kannamesai Festival|1=Kannamesai Festival}} (October 19) | location = {{ill|Toyokawachō|WD=Q17223204|1=Toyokawachō}} | coordinates = {{Coord|34.487583|N|136.699833|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} }} '''Kami-no-Mii Shrine''' (上御井神社) is a subordinate shrine of {{ill|Toyōke Daijingū|ja|豊受大神宮|1=Toyōke Daijingū|2=ja|3=豊受大神宮|4=zh|5=豐受大神宮|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyōke Daijingū|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} (Geku) within the {{ill|Ise Shrine|en|Ise Shrine|WD=Q687168|lt=Ise Grand Shrine|1=Ise Grand Shrine}}. It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|qq=}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo River Town|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q17223204}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|WD=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura-yama (Ise)|1=takakurayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11668766}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii Ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|WD=Q3078312|lt=wooden fences|1=Sacred fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|WD=Q178885|lt=kami|1=Deity}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|en|Takamagahara|WD=Q1141974|lt=Takamagahara|1=Takamagahara}} to {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|WD=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|WD=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q2983724}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|qq=}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|WD=Q872023|lt=JR Sangu Line|1=Sangū Line}} or {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|WD=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line|1=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q10884865}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mi Shrine|1=Q11488769|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11488769}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|上御井神社|version=97910291|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:上御井神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17226402}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water wells in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:pages]]

It is located within Geku’s precincts, specifically beyond the {{ill|Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|qq=}}, but it is in a restricted area, preventing general visitors from entering. Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo River Town|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17223204}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura-yama (Ise)|1=takakurayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668766}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii Ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|qid=Q3078312|lt=wooden fences|1=Sacred fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=kami|1=Deity}}. The nearby {{ill|Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|ja|下御井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Shimo-no-Mii Shrine|qq=}} serves as a backup well for Kami-no-Mii. Kami-no-Mii is said to have never dried up.[http://www.yoitokose.jp/ise/encyclopedia/s0003.php Shimo-no-Mii Shrine] - Ise Jingu Encyclopedia (retrieved September 11, 2013). However, the "Records of Omiya Affairs" (太神宮諸雑事記) notes that in 1050 (Eisho 5), Kami-no-Mii did dry up, prompting the use of water from Shimo-no-Mii. During such events, envoys were dispatched to the imperial court to pray, reflecting the well's sanctity. Every morning, priests draw water from Kami-no-Mii using long ladles while dressed in purified robes.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 Tradition dictates that priests must not see their reflection in the water.Sakurai (1998): p. 49 Additionally, the water used for brewing sacred sake at Ise Jingu is also drawn from this well.Kato (1979): p. 284 == History == The origin of Kami-no-Mii Shrine is unknown. Although the shrine is not listed in the "Toyuke-no-Miya Ritual Record," terms like "Well" (御井) and "Well deity" (御井神) are mentioned.Hozumi (2013): p. 162 The waka (Japanese poem) in the "Collection of Elegant Japanese Poems" (風雅和歌集) references Kami-no-Mii as follows: {{Cquote|Through the ages, endlessly drawn, this water will not run dry—Oshio-i, bestowed from the heavens.}} The poem refers to the legend of Oshio-i (忍穂井), brought down from {{ill|Takamagahara|en|Takamagahara|qid=Q1141974|lt=Takamagahara|1=Takamagahara}} to {{ill|Hyūga Province|en|Hyūga Province|qid=Q513737|lt=Hyūga Province|1=Hyūga Province}}, then to the heavenly spring of {{ill|Tanba Province|en|Tanba Province|qid=Q954511|lt=Tanba Province|1=Tanba Province}}. It was finally moved to Ise with the enshrinement of {{ill|Toyouke Omikami|ja|豊受大神|lt=Toyouke Ōmikami|1=Toyouke-hime|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke Omikami|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyouke-hime|ja_comment=jawiki redirects to トヨウケビメ|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q2983724}}. During the Edo period, rituals involving Kami-no-Mii were overseen by the priestly Watarai family. The well was protected under state supervision until 1872, when control shifted to the Meiji government, but it was reinstated under the shrine’s jurisdiction the following year.Sakurai (1991): pp. 266, 272 == Access == Since general access to Kami-no-Mii Shrine is prohibited, visitors may worship from a nearby spot beyond {{ill|Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Ise)|lt=Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|ja|大津神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ōtsu Shrine (Habikino) (Ise)|qq=}}. ; Public Transport * 5-minute walk south from {{ill|Sangū Line|en|Sangū Line|qid=Q872023|lt=JR Sangu Line|1=Sangū Line}} or {{ill|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|en|Yamada Line (Kintetsu)|qid=Q900630|lt=Kintetsu Yamada Line|1=Yamada Line (Kintetsu)}} Ise-shi Station. * 5-minute walk from the Mie Kotsu "Ise-shi Station" bus stop. * Immediate access from the Mie Kotsu "Geku-mae" bus stop. ; By Car * About 5 minutes north from {{ill|Ise Nishi Interchange|ja|伊勢西インターチェンジ|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Ise Nishi Interchange|1=Ise-nishi itnerchange|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q10884865}} on the Ise Expressway. == References == {{Reflist|2}} == Related Articles == * {{ill|List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|ja|神宮125社の一覧|lt=List of 125 Shrines of Ise|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|3=伊勢国の式内社一覧|1=List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/List of the 125 Shrines of Ise|13=User:Immanuelle/List of Shikinaisha in Ise Province|qq=}} * {{ill|Mi Shrine|ja|御井神社|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Mi Shrine|1=Q11488769|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11488769}} == External Links == * [http://www.jingukaikan.jp/125mairi/m02/0206.html Kami-no-Mii Shrine Official Page] - Ise Jingu Reverence Association {{125 Shrines of Ise}} {{translated page|ja|上御井神社|version=97910291|comment=Imported full ja history}} [[ja:上御井神社]] {{wikidata link|Q17226402}} [[Category:125 Shrines of Ise]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Ise City]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Mie Prefecture]] [[Category:Water wells in Japan]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

Therefore, worshippers must perform rituals from a designated prayer spot nearby.Ise Bunka-sha (2008): p. 37 [[File:Kaminomiinojinja.jpg|thumb|Kami-no-Mii Shrine
The roof of the enclosure can be seen from the entrance of Yamada Construction Area, though general access is prohibited.]] == Overview == Kami-no-Mii Shrine is located in {{ill|Toyo River Town|ja|豊川町 (伊勢市)|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Toyo River Town|1=Toyokawachō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q17223204}}, {{ill|Ise, Mie|en|Ise, Mie|qid=Q328067|lt=Ise, Mie|1=Ise, Mie}}, within the precincts of Geku, specifically at the base of Mount Fujioka, one of the peaks forming {{ill|Takakura-yama (Ise)|ja|高倉山|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Takakura-yama (Ise)|1=takakurayama|comment=changed title based on wikidata|qid=Q11668766}}.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): p. 17 Among the four subordinate shrines under Geku, Kami-no-Mii Ranks third.Ujiyamada City Hall (1929): pp. 15, 17 There is no honden (main shrine); instead, the sacred well, Kami-no-Mii, is covered by an enclosure called the "Well Enclosure" (御井覆屋), surrounded by double layers of {{ill|Tamagaki|en|Tamagaki|qid=Q3078312|lt=wooden fences|1=Sacred fences}}.Hozumi (2013): p. 163 The enshrined deity is the guardian deity of Kami-no-Mii (上御井鎮守神), worshipped as the protector of the sacred water used in Geku’s rituals or the well itself.Gakken Publishing (2013): p. 58 During Geku’s ceremonies, Kami-no-Mii Shrine also receives offerings.Sakurai (1998): p. 48 == Kami-no-Mii == Kami-no-Mii is a sacred well from which water is drawn for offering to the {{ill|Deity|en|Deity|qid=Q178885|lt=kami|1=Deity}}.
